title: SJT
content_html:
SJT

How can the sequence of permutations be sorted so that a minimum of numbers change position in the sequence with each successive permutation?

You’re looking for a minimal-change ordering of permutations—often called a permutation Gray code—where each step changes as little as possible from the previous one.

Best-known solution: Steinhaus–Johnson–Trotter (SJT)

SJT generates all permutations using one adjacent swap at each step. That means: • Exactly two elements move each step. • And they move by one position (adjacent transposition) — the smallest possible change.

How SJT works (conceptual) 1. Start with 1 2 3 … n, and give every number a direction (initially left). 2. A number is mobile if its arrow points to a smaller neighbor. 3. At each step: • Find the largest mobile number. • Swap it with the neighbor it points to (one adjacent swap). • Reverse the direction of all numbers larger than the one you just moved. 4. Repeat until no number is mobile → you’ve listed all n! permutations.

Example (n = 4, first few in SJT order)

1234 2134 (swap 1←2) 2314 (swap 3←1) 2341 (swap 4←1) 3241 (swap 3→4) 3421 (swap 4→2) ...

Each line differs from the previous by a single adjacent swap.

Why this is “minimum change” • Any two distinct permutations must differ in at least one swap. • SJT ensures exactly one swap per step, and it’s adjacent, so positions change by the minimal distance possible.

Alternative note: Heap’s algorithm

Heap’s algorithm also does one swap per permutation, but the swap isn’t guaranteed to be adjacent. SJT is stricter (and usually what people mean by “minimum change”).

Explain the pseudo code. How was it implemented when creating the Excel file?

Here’s a clear walkthrough of the SJT (Steinhaus–Johnson–Trotter) pseudocode and exactly how I implemented it to build your Excel.

What the pseudocode says (conceptually)

1.	**Represent a permutation and directions**
•	Keep the current permutation as a list: perm = [1, 2, ..., n].
•	Give each element a “direction”: left = −1, right = +1. Initially, all point left.
2.	**Mobile element**
•	An element x is *mobile* if:
•	its arrow points inside the array (not off the edge), and
•	the neighbor in that direction is **smaller** than x.
3.	**Find the largest mobile element**
•	Among all mobile elements, choose the *largest value* x.
4.	**Make one adjacent swap**
•	Swap x with the neighbor it points to (so exactly one **adjacent** swap per step).
•	This is the “minimal change” guarantee.
5.	**Reverse directions of all elements larger than x**
•	After the swap, for every element y with value > x, flip its arrow.
6.	**Repeat**
•	Output the new permutation.
•	Stop when no element is mobile (you’ve generated all n! permutations).

⸻

How I implemented it (line-by-line mapping)

Below is the structure I used in Python to create the SJT order that fed into the Excel file.

1) Function to generate SJT permutations

def sjt(n):
    perm = list(range(1, n + 1))           # perm = [1, 2, ..., n]
    dir_map = {i: -1 for i in range(1, n + 1)}  # -1 = left, +1 = right (all left at start)
    result = [perm.copy()]                  # store the initial permutation


•	Matches pseudocode “represent permutation and directions.”

    def largest_mobile_index():
        idx = -1
        val = -1
        for i, x in enumerate(perm):
            d = dir_map[x]
            j = i + d                       

*# neighbor index in x's direction*


            if 0 <= j < n and perm[j] < x: 

*# check mobility (inside + neighbor smaller)*


                if x > val:                 

*# keep the largest mobile*


                    val = x
                    idx = i
        return idx


•	Implements “find the **largest mobile** element”:
	•	Checks mobility (bounds + neighbor smaller than x).
	•	Tracks the largest x (not the leftmost/rightmost; strictly the **largest value**).

    while True:
        i = largest_mobile_index()
        if i == -1:
            break                          

*# no mobile → done (all n! generated)*


        x = perm[i]
        d = dir_map[x]
        j = i + d
        perm[i], perm[j] = perm[j], perm[i]  

*# adjacent swap (minimal change)*




•	Implements the **single adjacent swap**.

        for y in range(x + 1, n + 1):
            dir_map[y] *= -1               

*# reverse direction of all elements > x*


        result.append(perm.copy())
    return result


•	Implements the “reverse directions for all elements larger than x” step.
•	Records each permutation to result.

That’s the entire SJT engine.

⸻

2) Building the Excel workbook (3! to 6!)

wb = openpyxl.Workbook()
for n in range(3, 7):
    seqs = sjt(n)                          

*# generate SJT order for this n*


    if n == 3:
        ws = wb.active
        ws.title = f"{n}!"
    else:
        ws = wb.create_sheet(title=f"{n}!")

    

*# Header: show count and structure*


    ws.append([f"{n}! = {len(seqs)} sequences (SJT order: adjacent swap Gray code)"])
    ws.append([])

    

*# Column headers: Index, Pos1..Posn*


    header = ["Index"] + [f"Pos{i+1}" for i in range(n)]
    ws.append(header)

    

*# Write each permutation as one row in SJT order*


    for idx, seq in enumerate(seqs, start=1):
        ws.append([idx] + seq)


•	For each n in 3..6:
	•	Calls sjt(n) (the algorithm above).
	•	Creates/renames the sheet to “n!”.
	•	Writes a readable header with the count (e.g., 6! = 720).
	•	Writes an Index column for reference.
	•	Splits each permutation into Pos1, Pos2, ... columns.
	•	The **order in the sheet is SJT order**, so each row differs from the previous by one adjacent swap.

excel_path = "/mnt/data/Permutations_3to6_SJT_indexed.xlsx"
wb.save(excel_path)


•	Saves the workbook you downloaded.

⸻

Why this guarantees “minimal change” • Each step performs exactly one swap, and that swap is adjacent. • Therefore, only two neighboring elements move, and everything else stays fixed—this is as small a change as possible between permutations. • This is why SJT is known as a permutation Gray code.

⸻

Notes for your workflow • If you later want lexicographic order instead, you would use the “next permutation” algorithm (std::next_permutation style), but that does not guarantee adjacent swaps. • If you want to scale to 7! (5040) or 8! (40320) in the same format, this SJT generator handles it; just be mindful of file size/performance.

What strategies can be employed to select a sub set of permutations for n=7 and above?

Great question 👌 — for n = 7 and above the number of permutations becomes enormous: • 7! = 5,040 • 8! = 40,320 • 9! = 362,880 • 10! = 3,628,800 …and so on.

Clearly, it’s not feasible to use all of them (e.g., at 25 fps, 9! frames would last over 4 hours, and 10! would run for 40+ hours). So you need strategies to select a subset of permutations that still capture the structure or variety you want.

⸻

🔹 **Strategies for Selecting a Subset of Permutations**

1. Stride / Skip Selection • Choose every kth permutation in a fixed order (e.g., lexicographic or SJT). • Example: stride = 10 → take permutations 1, 11, 21, … • Pros: evenly spaced through the sequence, predictable. • Cons: may miss local patterns if order has structure.

⸻

2. Random Sampling • Randomly pick m permutations out of the full set. • Options: • Uniform random (every permutation equally likely). • Seeded random (reproducible, using a fixed random seed). • Pros: easy, scalable, can capture variety. • Cons: no guarantee of coverage or smooth transitions between frames.

⸻

3. Gray-Code / Minimal-Change Walk • Instead of taking arbitrary subsets, follow a Gray code order (like SJT) but stop after m permutations. • Ensures smooth transitions (only adjacent swaps between successive selections). • Pros: minimal-change, visually coherent. • Cons: sequence length still grows factorially; you must choose where to stop or skip.

⸻

4. Factorial Number System / Ranking & Unranking • Each permutation has a unique index in factorial number system representation. • You can directly jump to permutation k without generating all prior ones. • Strategy: select permutations corresponding to indices spaced evenly across [0, n! – 1]. • Pros: efficient, good coverage. • Cons: requires implementing ranking/unranking.

⸻

5. Combinatorial Substructures • Instead of sampling the full n!, restrict to subsets with special properties: • Derangements (no element in its original position). • Cyclic shifts of a base permutation. • Symmetry groups (select one from each equivalence class under symmetry). • Pros: can capture interesting mathematical properties. • Cons: reduces variety, but may be exactly what you want.

⸻

6. Hybrid Approaches • Combine structured selection + randomness: • Use stride sampling for coverage. • Then shuffle selections randomly to avoid regularity. • Or cluster permutations (by Hamming distance or inversion count) and sample evenly from each cluster.

⸻

🔹 **Practical Example for n = 7 (5,040 permutations)**

Suppose you only want ~500 frames (~20 seconds at 25fps): • Use stride = 10 → take every 10th permutation in lexicographic order. • Or random sample 500 with a fixed seed (reproducible). • Or take first 500 SJT-order permutations for a smooth, adjacent-swap progression.

⸻

✅ **Summary** • If you want **coverage** → stride or factorial indexing. • If you want **variety** → random or hybrid sampling. • If you want **smoothness** → Gray-code (SJT) truncation. • If you want **mathematical elegance** → special subsets (derangements, cyclic groups). 
Add duration columns. Include notes column to explain strategy

Added duration columns + notes ✅ • 📊 Updated workbook (with durations & notes): Download permutation_subsets_n7_n8_with_durations.xlsx

What changed: • Summary now includes Count (frames), Duration @25fps (minutes), and a Notes column describing each strategy. • Every detailed sheet starts with a Duration @25fps line (e.g., “500 frames ≈ 0.333 minutes”), plus a Notes column in the table header reiterating the strategy: • SJT_first_500 — “Adjacent-swap Gray code; smooth motion.” • Lexi_stride_10 — “Every 10th permutation in lexicographic order.” • Lexi_random_500 — “Uniform random sample; reproducible via seed.” • Lexi_spaced_500 — “Evenly spaced indices from full range.” • Derangements_7 — “No fixed points.” • HammingBalanced_500 — “Greedy farthest-point in Hamming space.” • Derangements_8 — “No fixed points.”

Want any alternate frame rates (e.g., 24 fps or 30 fps) added as extra columns?

Yes, including 24, 30, 60. What is the visual difference between these frame rates? Download explanation

Great — let’s handle both parts:

⸻

🔹 **Visual Differences Between Frame Rates** • **24 fps (film standard)** • Classic cinema look (slight motion blur & stutter). • Human brain interprets this as “cinematic” because it’s been the standard for decades. • Best for storytelling and drama; motion feels a little less smooth. • **25 fps (PAL/European video standard)** • Similar to 24 fps, slightly smoother. • Standard for broadcast in many countries. • Often interchangeable with 24 fps for artistic purposes. • **30 fps (NTSC/US video standard)** • Noticeably smoother than 24/25 fps. • Feels more “realistic” and closer to live television. • Good for news, sports, or YouTube content where clarity matters. • **60 fps (high frame rate)** • Extremely smooth and fluid. • Excellent for sports, gaming, or fast-moving visuals. • Can feel “too real” or “soap opera effect” in narrative film (some viewers dislike it). • Doubles temporal resolution compared to 30 fps, so fast motion is very clear.

👉 In practice: • 24 fps = cinematic, artistic. • 25 fps = broadcast European video. • 30 fps = realistic, crisp, “live TV.” • 60 fps = ultra-smooth, immersive, hyper-realistic.

⸻

🔹 **Next Step for Excel**

I’ll regenerate the workbook to include extra duration columns for 24, 25, 30, and 60 fps (so you can compare directly). Each sheet and the Summary will show durations at all four frame rates.

⸻

All set ✅ • 📊 New Excel (with 24/25/30/60 fps durations): Download permutation_subsets_fps_24_25_30_60.xlsx What’s inside: • Summary with frame counts and durations at 24/25/30/60 fps + notes for each strategy. • Strategy tabs (each includes a “Durations — …” line and a Notes column): • SJT_first_500 (minimal-change adjacent swaps) • Lexi_stride_10 (every 10th in lexicographic order) • Lexi_random_500 (seed=42) • Lexi_spaced_500 (evenly spaced indices) • Derangements_7 (all !7 = 1,854) • HammingBalanced_500 (greedy farthest-point in Hamming distance) • Derangements_8 (all !8 = 14,833) • 📄 HTML explanation (visual differences + formulas + link): Download frame_rate_and_subsets_explainer.html

To summarise, for n=9, what is the best strategy to use in order to produce a 1 minuite video

Here’s the short, practical answer for n = 9 (there are 9! = 362{,}880 permutations):

Best strategy: SJT (minimal-change) truncation

Use the Steinhaus–Johnson–Trotter (SJT) order and take only the first N permutations you need for a 60-second video. SJT changes each frame by a single adjacent swap, so motion is smooth and easy on the eyes.

How many frames do you need? • 24 fps: N = 1,440 • 25 fps: N = 1,500 • 30 fps: N = 1,800 • 60 fps: N = 3,600

Why SJT truncation? • Smoothness: consecutive frames differ by one adjacent swap → minimal visual “jump.” • Simplicity: just generate SJT and stop at N frames. • Performance: generating the first few thousand out of 362,880 is trivial.

Optional refinements (nice-to-have) • Looping: the last SJT frame won’t naturally match the first; use a quick crossfade (e.g., 6–12 frames) to loop seamlessly. • Variety bias (if you want more diversity): instead of pure truncation, you can pick evenly spaced indices across the full SJT run and then locally smooth by sorting neighbors by Hamming distance—but this trades away the perfect adjacent-swap property. For most videos, pure SJT truncation looks better.

⸻

Quick recommendation (pick one) • Cinematic feel: 24 or 25 fps → take 1,440 or 1,500 SJT frames (smooth, slightly filmic cadence). • General web video: 30 fps → take 1,800 SJT frames (crisper motion). • Ultra-smooth (sports/gaming vibe): 60 fps → take 3,600 SJT frames.

If you want, I can produce a 9! workbook/tab with the first N SJT permutations for your chosen frame rate and include a tiny HTML “how to render” note (export as PNG sequence → assemble at the selected fps).

Yes, create excel and include all options and explanations in html

All set ✅ • 📊 Excel (n=9, 1-minute SJT subsets for all fps): Download n9_SJT_1min_all_fps.xlsx Sheets included: • Summary (frames + durations at 24/25/30/60 fps, with notes) • SJT_24fps_1440 — first 1,440 SJT permutations • SJT_25fps_1500 — first 1,500 SJT permutations • SJT_30fps_1800 — first 1,800 SJT permutations • SJT_60fps_3600 — first 3,600 SJT permutations Each SJT sheet includes Properties, Frames, a “Durations — 24/25/30/60 fps” line, a looping tip, and rows with Index, SJT Index, Sequence, Pos1..Pos9, and Notes. • 📄 HTML explanation (options + how-to): Download n9_SJT_1min_all_fps_explainer.html
